section.title: The Man with the Measuring Line
section.sectionRationale: The third night vision, followed by a joyful oracle. A man goes to measure Jerusalem, but the word comes that the city will overflow its walls; God Himself will be a wall of fire around it and the glory within it. The vision then opens outward: exiles are called to flee Babylon, the nations will be judged, many will join themselves to the LORD, and He will come to dwell among His people.
section.thesis: God's restored city cannot be contained by walls, for He Himself is her wall of fire and her glory; He will judge those who harmed her, gather the nations to Himself, and come to dwell in her midst.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the measuring line and the promise of a city too full for walls; the wall of fire and the call to flee Babylon, for whoever touches Zion touches the apple of God's eye; and the joyful promise that God will dwell among His people and many nations will join the LORD.

verses.text: Then I lifted up my eyes and saw a man with a {{qav|measuring line}} in his hand.
“Where are you going?” I asked. “To measure Jerusalem,” he replied, “and to determine its width and length.”
Then the angel who was speaking with me went forth, and another angel came forward to meet him
and said to him, “Run and tell that young man: ‘Jerusalem will be a city without walls because of the multitude of men and livestock within it.
For I will be a wall of fire around it, declares the LORD, and I will be the {{kavod|glory}} within it.’”
“Get up! Get up! Flee from the land of the north,” declares the LORD, “for I have scattered you like the four winds of heaven,” declares the LORD.
“Get up, O Zion! Escape, you who dwell with the Daughter of Babylon!”
For this is what the LORD of Hosts says: “After His Glory has sent Me against the nations that have plundered you, for whoever touches you touches the {{babah|apple}} of His eye,
I will surely wave My hand over them, so that they will become plunder for their own servants. Then you will know that the LORD of Hosts has sent Me.”

units.label: God will dwell among you
units.note: many nations joined to the LORD

verses.text: “Shout for joy and be glad, O Daughter of Zion, for I am coming to {{shakan|dwell}} among you,” declares the LORD.
“On that day many nations will join themselves to the LORD, and they will become My people. I will dwell among you, and you will know that the LORD of Hosts has sent Me to you.
And the LORD will take possession of Judah as His portion in the Holy Land, and He will once again choose Jerusalem.
Be silent before the LORD, all people, for He has roused Himself from His holy dwelling.”

qav.gloss: A measuring line or cord, used to lay out the dimensions of a building or city. In 1:16 a measuring line over Jerusalem signaled restoration; here the young surveyor's line is gently overruled, because the coming city will be too full of people to be bounded by measured walls.

kavod.gloss: Glory, weight, honor, the visible radiance of God's presence that once filled the tabernacle and temple. God promises to be the glory within Jerusalem, her true protection and splendor, greater than any wall or building. This glory is fulfilled when the Word dwells among us in Christ.

babah.gloss: The pupil, the gate or little one of the eye, the most tender and fiercely guarded part of the body. To say that whoever touches God's people touches the apple of His eye is to declare how precious and protected they are; an attack on them is felt by God as an attack on Himself.

shakan.gloss: To dwell, settle, take up residence. It is the word behind the tabernacle and the later term Shekinah, the dwelling presence of God. God's promise to shakan among His people is the heart of the covenant, fulfilled when God dwells with us in Christ and consummated when He dwells with humanity forever.

basic.context: This is the third night vision, and the most joyful so far. A young man sets out to measure Jerusalem, but God stops the survey: the restored city will be so full of people that walls could never contain it. Instead God Himself will be its wall of fire and its glory. The vision then calls exiles still in Babylon to come home and promises that God will dwell among His people and draw in the nations.
basic.hermeneutics: This is vision plus oracle, so we read it for its promise rather than as a building plan. People ask how much refers to the second temple period and how much looks ahead to the last days when nations stream to God. The best answer is both: it begins to come true then and reaches its fullness in Christ and the new Jerusalem.
basic.originalLanguages: The man carries a qav, a measuring line, but the city will outgrow it. God promises to be the kavod, the glory, within Jerusalem. His people are the babah, the apple or pupil of His eye, fiercely guarded. And He pledges to shakan, to dwell, among them, the covenant word behind tabernacle and Shekinah.
basic.typology: God as the glory within Jerusalem points to Christ, in whom the glory of God dwells bodily. God coming to dwell among His people is fulfilled when the Word becomes flesh and tabernacles with us, and finally when God dwells with humanity in the new creation. The gathering of many nations foreshadows the church.
basic.christ: I am coming to dwell among you is answered supremely in Jesus, Immanuel, in whom God dwells with us. He is the glory of God, and through Him many nations are joined to the LORD and become His people. The city too full for walls is the growing people of God drawn from every nation.
basic.god: God is the true security and splendor of His people, better than any wall. He counts them as precious as the pupil of His own eye and takes their afflictions personally. He is holy and sovereign, roused from His dwelling to act, yet He delights to come near and dwell among the humble.
basic.crossReferences: John 1 says the Word dwelt among us and we beheld His glory. Revelation 21 shows the new Jerusalem without a temple, for God is its glory, and nations walk in its light. Deuteronomy 32 calls Israel the apple of His eye. Isaiah 2 and 60 foretell nations streaming to Zion.
basic.oneStory: The third vision opens the story outward: God's city cannot be contained, for He is her wall and glory, and He is coming to dwell among a people gathered from all nations. This runs straight to Christ, God dwelling with us, and on to the new Jerusalem where God dwells with humanity forever.

context.literary: The third of the eight night visions, expanded by a joyful oracle in verses 6 to 13. It builds on the first two: the first promised a measuring line stretched over Jerusalem, the second cast down the horns, and now the third shows the measured city overflowing. The oracle shifts from vision to direct address, calling exiles home and summoning the nations, and closes with a hush, be silent before the LORD.
context.historical: Jerusalem in 519 BC was a small, sparsely populated town with broken walls, and many Jews still remained in Babylon, the land of the north, comfortable and reluctant to return. The vision speaks against both the smallness of the present city and the inertia of those who stayed, promising a future so full and secure that it summons them to come home.
context.canonical: The promise of God dwelling among His people gathers up the tabernacle and temple traditions and reaches toward the incarnation and the new creation. The vision of many nations joining the LORD stands with Isaiah's picture of the nations streaming to Zion, and its imagery is taken up directly in Revelation's vision of the new Jerusalem, a city whose glory is God Himself.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lift the eyes of a small, walled-in remnant to a future far larger than they imagine, a city overflowing with people, protected and glorified by God's own presence, drawing in the nations, so that they rejoice, come home from Babylon, and trust the God who is coming to dwell among them.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The visions and promises are description of what God will do; the calls to flee Babylon, shout for joy, and be silent are exhortation. The abiding truths are God's presence as His people's true security, their preciousness to Him, and His purpose to gather the nations and dwell among them.

debates.question: Does the promise of an unwalled, overflowing city refer to the post-exilic period or the messianic age?

views.label: Ultimately the messianic and eternal age
views.case: The scale of the promise, many nations joined to the LORD and God dwelling in the midst, outruns anything the second temple period saw and finds fullness in Christ and the new Jerusalem of Revelation 21.

views.label: Beginning then and expanding toward the end
views.case: The promise genuinely encouraged the returning remnant and began to unfold in their day, then widens through the gospel to its consummation, so present and future are held together.

debates.bearing: Either way the hope is God's presence and the ingathering of the nations, realized in Christ; the debate concerns timing, not the substance of the promise.

debates.question: Who is the Me sent against the nations in verses 8 to 9 and 11?

views.label: The angel of the LORD, the divine messenger
views.case: The speaker is sent by the LORD of Hosts yet acts with divine authority and is Himself the LORD, the mediating figure seen through the visions, whom many read as a pre-incarnate appearing of the Son.

views.label: The prophet or an angelic agent commissioned by God
views.case: The one sent may be understood as God's appointed representative carrying out His judgment on the nations and His salvation of Zion.

debates.bearing: Both readings keep God as the initiator and guarantee; the sent one accomplishes God's purpose, and the Christian sees the pattern fulfilled in the Son sent by the Father.

study.originalLanguages: Four words open the vision's meaning. The young man carries a qav, a measuring line, ready to fix Jerusalem's dimensions, but the promise overrules the survey: the city will be too full of people for walls. In place of walls God pledges Himself twice over: a wall of fire around, and the kavod, the glory, within, the weighty radiance of His presence that once filled the tabernacle and temple. His people are the babah, the pupil of His eye, the tenderest, most fiercely guarded part of the body, so that any who strike them strike at God Himself. And the climax is shakan, to dwell, the covenant word behind the tabernacle and the later Shekinah: I am coming to dwell among you, the promise fulfilled when the Word becomes flesh and dwells among us and consummated when God dwells with humanity forever.

typology.type: God as the glory within Jerusalem
typology.fulfillment: Christ, in whom the fullness of God's glory dwells bodily, John 1:14 and Colossians 2:9.

typology.type: I am coming to dwell among you
typology.fulfillment: The incarnation, God with us in Christ, and God's final dwelling with humanity, Matthew 1:23 and Revelation 21:3.

typology.type: Many nations joined to the LORD as His people
typology.fulfillment: The church gathered from every nation, and the nations walking by the light of the new Jerusalem, Ephesians 2:12-13 and Revelation 21:24.

study.christ: The two great promises of this vision, God as the glory within His city and God coming to dwell among His people, meet in Jesus Christ. John's Gospel deliberately echoes this language: the Word became flesh and dwelt, tabernacled, among us, and we beheld His glory, the glory of the only Son. In Christ the kavod that Zechariah promised is not a cloud filling a temple but a person, God with us. Through Him the promise that many nations will join themselves to the LORD comes true as the gospel gathers a people from every tribe and tongue, so that the city too full for walls is the countless people of God. And the whole hope points on to Revelation's new Jerusalem, a city with no temple, for the Lord God is its temple and the Lamb its lamp, where God dwells with His people forever.
study.god: This vision reveals a God whose presence is His people's true wall and true splendor. He does not merely provide security; He is the security, a wall of fire that no enemy can breach, and the glory that makes the city radiant from within. He holds His people so dear that He calls them the pupil of His own eye and treats every assault on them as an assault on Himself. He is holy and sovereign, roused from His holy dwelling to act, before whom all flesh must fall silent. Yet this exalted God stoops to dwell among the humble and to gather the nations, revealing that His deepest purpose is not distance but nearness, to be their God in their midst.

crossReferences.note: The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory, fulfilling I am coming to dwell among you and I will be the glory within it.
God dwells with His people in the new Jerusalem, a city needing no temple, its glory God Himself, with the nations walking in its light.
God kept Israel as the apple of His eye, the same tender image of His fierce protection.
The glory of the LORD rises upon Zion and nations come to her light, paralleling the glory within and the nations joined to the LORD.

study.oneStory: The third night vision breaks Jerusalem open: no wall can contain the city God is building, for He is her wall of fire and her glory, and He is coming to dwell in her midst with a people drawn from all nations. That promise runs straight through the incarnation, where the Word dwells among us in glory, into the church gathered from every tongue, and on to the new Jerusalem where God dwells with humanity forever. The story is moving toward the day when heaven's silence breaks and God takes up residence with His people for good.

quiz.q: Why will Jerusalem be a city without walls?

options.text: Because it will overflow with so many people and livestock, and God Himself will be its wall of fire.
options.note: Verses 4-5 promise a city too full for walls, protected by God's own presence.

options.text: Because the people will be too poor to build walls.
options.note: The absence of walls is a sign of abundance and divine protection, not poverty.

options.text: Because walls are forbidden in the Holy Land.
options.note: There is no such prohibition; the point is God's presence surpassing walls.

options.text: Because the city will be abandoned.
options.note: The city overflows with life, the opposite of abandonment.

quiz.q: What does it mean that whoever touches God's people touches the apple of His eye?

options.text: God counts His people as supremely precious and takes any attack on them as an attack on Himself.
options.note: The pupil of the eye is the most tender, fiercely guarded part; God guards His people so.

options.text: God is easily irritated by minor offenses.
options.note: The image is about preciousness and protection, not petty irritation.

options.text: God keeps His distance from His people's troubles.
options.note: The opposite: He feels their afflictions as His own.

options.text: God only protects the strongest among His people.
options.note: He guards all His people as the apple of His eye, not just the strong.

quiz.q: What is the climactic promise of this vision?

options.text: I am coming to dwell among you, with many nations joined to the LORD as His people.
options.note: Verses 10-11 promise God's dwelling presence and the ingathering of the nations.

options.text: That Jerusalem will finally get high stone walls.
options.note: The promise is God's presence surpassing walls, not stronger fortifications.

options.text: That the nations will be shut out forever.
options.note: Many nations will join themselves to the LORD, not be excluded.

options.text: That God will remain far off in heaven.
options.note: The promise is precisely that God will come to dwell among His people.

quiz.q: How is the promise God as the glory within it fulfilled in Christ?

options.text: In Jesus the glory of God dwells bodily, the Word made flesh who tabernacled among us.
options.note: John 1:14 and Colossians 2:9 show Christ as the glory of God dwelling with His people.

options.text: It is never fulfilled and remains only a wish.
options.note: The New Testament presents it as fulfilled in Christ and consummated in the new Jerusalem.

options.text: It refers only to gold decorating the temple.
options.note: The glory is God's own presence, not mere ornamentation.

options.text: It means God's glory left His people for good.
options.note: The promise is God's glory coming to dwell within, fulfilled in Christ.

quiz.q: Why are exiles told to flee from the land of the north?

options.text: God is calling His scattered people home to the restored city where He will dwell.
options.note: Verses 6-7 summon those still in Babylon to return to Zion.

options.text: Because the north was more comfortable and they should stay.
options.note: They are told to leave the comfort of Babylon, not remain in it.

options.text: Because God had permanently rejected them there.
options.note: God calls them back in love, not final rejection.

options.text: Because Jerusalem had been destroyed again.
options.note: Jerusalem is being restored, which is why they are called home.

questions: God promised to be a wall of fire around His people rather than relying on stone. Where are you tempted to trust in walls of your own making instead of God's presence?
God calls His people the apple of His eye. How does it change your sense of worth to know that God guards you as tenderly as the pupil of His own eye?
Exiles were told to get up and flee comfortable Babylon to come home. Is there a comfortable place God is calling you to leave in order to follow Him more fully?
The vision promises a city too full for walls, gathered from many nations. How does the worldwide scope of God's people enlarge your vision of the church?
I am coming to dwell among you is fulfilled in Christ, God with us. How does knowing God has come near, and will dwell with you forever, steady you today?

translationNotes.note: Glory renders kavod, the weighty radiance of God's presence; God promises to be Jerusalem's protection and splendor, language John applies to Christ dwelling among us.

Apple of His eye renders a phrase for the pupil, the most tender and guarded part of the eye; a footnote notes the alternative the pupil. The clause After His Glory has sent Me is difficult and variously rendered, as the footnote shows.
Dwell renders shakan, the covenant word behind the tabernacle and the later term Shekinah, the settled presence of God among His people.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zechariah 2:1-13 (Hebrew 2:5-17). All 13 verses reproduced in order. No em dashes present in this passage.
